title_en: Treatise on the Jade Mechanism and the True Viscera
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: The spring pulse is like a bowstring — in what way is it like a bowstring? Qibo replied: The spring pulse corresponds to the liver, to wood of the east, the direction by which the myriad things begin to be born. Therefore its qi comes soft and weak, light and empty, yet smooth, straight and upright, and long — hence it is called "bowstring-like." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes full and forceful, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if the qi comes insubstantial and faint, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the spring pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes a person forgetful, dazed and dizzy, with disorders of the crown of the head; in insufficiency, it makes the chest ache, drawing into the back, and below causes fullness in both flanks and the sides beneath the ribs. Huangdi said: Good.

The summer pulse is like a hook — in what way is it like a hook? Qibo said: The summer pulse corresponds to the heart, to fire of the south, the direction by which the myriad things flourish and grow. Therefore its qi comes abundant and departs in decline — hence it is called "hook-like." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes abundant and also departs abundant, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if the qi comes not abundant but departs abundant instead, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the summer pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes the body hot and the skin ache, producing spreading sores; in insufficiency, it makes the heart vexed, with coughing and spitting appearing above, and an outflow of qi below. Huangdi said: Good.

The autumn pulse is like a floating pulse — in what way is it floating? Qibo said: The autumn pulse corresponds to the lung, to metal of the west, the direction by which the myriad things are gathered in and brought to completion. Therefore its qi comes light, empty, and floating, arriving quickly and dispersing as it leaves — hence it is called "floating." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes feather-like yet firm at the center and empty on both sides, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if the qi comes feather-like and faint, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the autumn pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes the qi rebel upward and the back ache, with a stifled, brooding feeling; in insufficiency, it makes a person pant, breathe with insufficient qi, and cough, with blood appearing when qi rises, and an abnormal sound heard below. Huangdi said: Good.

The winter pulse is like the "ying" [entrenched] pulse — in what way is it entrenched? Qibo said: The winter pulse corresponds to the kidney, to water of the north, the direction by which the myriad things are gathered together and stored away. Therefore its qi comes deep and beats hard — hence it is called "entrenched." Anything contrary to this is disease. Huangdi said: What is meant by contrary to this? Qibo said: If the qi comes like a stone flicked by the finger, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if it departs in a hurried, racing manner, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: In excess and insufficiency of the winter pulse, what are the diseases like in each case? Qibo said: In excess, it makes a person listless and weak, with pain along the spine and shortness of qi so that one does not wish to speak; in insufficiency, it makes the heart feel suspended as if famished, coldness in the region below the ribs, pain in the spine, fullness in the lower abdomen, and abnormal urination. Huangdi said: Good.

Huangdi said: The order of the four seasons and the changes of compliance and rebellion follow this pattern — but what alone governs the spleen pulse? Qibo said: The spleen pulse corresponds to earth, the solitary viscus that irrigates the four surrounding [viscera]. Huangdi said: Then can the good and the ill of the spleen be observed? Qibo said: The good cannot be seen; the ill can be seen. Huangdi said: In what way can the ill be seen? Qibo said: If it comes like flowing water, this is called excess, and the disease is on the outside; if it is like the beak of a bird, this is called insufficiency, and the disease is on the inside. Huangdi said: Master, you say the spleen is the solitary viscus, earth at the center that irrigates the four surrounding [viscera] — what are the diseases of its excess and insufficiency like? Qibo said: In excess, it makes the four limbs unable to be raised; in insufficiency, it makes the nine orifices fail to be free, called "doubled stiffness." Huangdi rose in astonishment, bowed twice, touched his head to the ground, and said: Excellent. I have grasped the great essentials of the pulse, the utmost calculations of the world, the transformations of the five colors and the pulse, the estimation of the ordinary and the extraordinary. The Way lies in unity: the spirit turns without reversing; if it reverses, it no longer turns, and then the pivotal moment is lost. The essentials of these utmost calculations are close at hand yet subtle. Let them be inscribed on jade tablets and stored in the treasury of the viscera and bowels, to be read every morning — this is called "the Jade Mechanism."

Each viscus receives qi from the viscus that generates it, transmits it to the viscus it overcomes, and its qi lodges in the viscus that generates it, but death occurs at the viscus that cannot overcome it. When a disease is about to bring death, it must first be transmitted onward until it reaches the viscus that cannot overcome it, and only then does death occur. This describes the reverse movement of qi, and hence death results. The liver receives qi from the heart, transmits it to the spleen, and its qi lodges in the kidney; death occurs at the lung. The heart receives qi from the spleen, transmits it to the lung, and its qi lodges in the liver; death occurs at the kidney. The spleen receives qi from the lung, transmits it to the kidney, and its qi lodges in the heart; death occurs at the liver. The lung receives qi from the kidney, transmits it to the liver, and its qi lodges in the spleen; death occurs at the heart. The kidney receives qi from the liver, transmits it to the heart, and its qi lodges in the lung; death occurs at the spleen. These are all cases of death from reverse transmission. Dividing a day and night into five parts allows one to divine the early or late hour of death.

Huangdi said: The five viscera communicate with one another, and their transmission always follows a sequence. When one of the five viscera has disease, it transmits the disease to the one it overcomes. If left untreated, the rule is that in three months or six months, or in three days or six days, the disease will have been transmitted through the five viscera and death will occur — this is the sequence of transmission following the order of overcoming. Hence it is said: by distinguishing what pertains to yang, one knows whence the disease has come; by distinguishing what pertains to yin, one knows the time of death or survival. This means knowing that the patient dies upon reaching the viscus that constrains him.

Therefore wind is the chief of the hundred diseases. Now when wind and cold lodge in a person, they make the fine body hairs stand erect, close the skin, and produce heat; at this time, one may induce sweating to release it, or, if there is numbness, insensitivity, swelling and pain, one may use hot decoctions, fomentation, moxibustion, or needling to remove it. If left untreated, the disease enters and lodges in the lung, called "lung bi [impediment]," bringing on cough and rising qi. If left untreated, the lung then transmits it onward to the liver, and the disease is called "liver bi," also called "reversal," with rib-side pain and the bringing up of food; at this time, one may treat by pressing or by needling. If left untreated, the liver transmits it to the spleen, and the disease is called "spleen wind," bringing on jaundice, heat in the abdomen, a vexed heart, and yellow discharge; at this time, one may treat by pressing, by medicine, or by bathing. If left untreated, the spleen transmits it to the kidney, and the disease is called "mounting-conglomeration," with oppressive heat and pain in the lower abdomen and a whitish discharge, also called "gu [parasitic] disease"; at this time, one may treat by pressing or by medicine. If left untreated, the kidney transmits it to the heart, and the sinews and vessels pull against one another and grow tense — the disease is called "convulsion"; at this time, one may treat by moxibustion or by medicine. If left untreated, when ten full days have passed, by rule death should occur. Alternatively, the kidney may transmit it to the heart, and the heart in turn transmits it back to the lung, bringing on chills and fever, and by rule death should occur within three years — this is the sequence of the disease.

However, when a disease breaks out suddenly, it need not follow this order of transmission; or its transmission and transformation may fail to follow the sequence — when it does not enter in sequence, it is because worry, fear, sorrow, joy, and anger prevent it from following its proper order, and so a person develops serious illness. Thus, if joy causes great depletion, then kidney qi will take advantage of the weakness; anger causes liver qi to take advantage; sorrow causes lung qi to take advantage; fear causes spleen qi to take advantage; worry causes heart qi to take advantage — this is the principle involved. Hence there are five types of disease, and five times five makes twenty-five variations, along with their transmissions and transformations. "Transmission" here is another name for "taking advantage of weakness."

When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, and this disturbance of qi is visible in the body's movement, death is expected within six months; when the true visceral pulse appears, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, and there is internal pain drawing into the shoulders and neck, death is expected within one month; when the true viscus pulse appears, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, and there is internal pain drawing into the shoulders and neck, death is expected within one month; when the true viscus pulse appears, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, breathing becomes labored, there is internal pain drawing into the shoulders and neck, the body is hot, the flesh wastes and the muscle masses break down, when the true viscus pulse appears, death occurs within ten months. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, the marrow within the shoulder wastes away, movement grows ever more feeble, when the true viscus pulse comes and appears, death is expected within one year; once the true viscus pulse is seen, then the day can be predicted. When the great bones become withered, the great flesh sinks and falls away, qi fills the chest, there is pain within the abdomen, discomfort in the heart region, heat in the shoulders, neck and body, the muscle masses break down and the flesh wastes, the eye sockets sink in, when the true viscus pulse appears and the eyes no longer recognize people, death is immediate; if the eyes still recognize people, death occurs when the season arrives that the affected viscus cannot overcome. In cases of urgent depletion where collapse strikes the body suddenly, the five viscera become sealed and closed off, the pathways of the pulse are blocked, and qi no longer comes and goes — this is like falling and drowning, and no fixed time of death can be given. When the pulse is cut off and does not come, or when a person's breath produces five or six beats of the pulse in a single respiration, yet the flesh and muscle have not wasted away, then even though the true visceral pulse does not appear, death will still occur. When the true liver pulse arrives, it is tense both within and without, like running one's finger along the edge of a blade, hard and unyielding, like pressing on the strings of a zither or lute; the complexion is greenish-white and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true heart pulse arrives, it is hard and pounding, like fingering the beads of Job's-tears seed strung one after another; the complexion is reddish-black and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true lung pulse arrives, it is large and empty, like feathers brushing against a person's skin; the complexion is whitish-red and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true kidney pulse arrives, it pounds and then breaks off, like a finger flicking a stone, hard and abrupt; the complexion is blackish-yellow and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. When the true spleen pulse arrives, it is weak, now rapid and now sparse; the complexion is yellowish-green and without luster, the body hair breaks off — then death occurs. Whenever any of the true visceral pulses appear, death follows in every case, and there is no treating it. Huangdi said: Why is it said that the appearance of the true visceral pulse means death? Qibo said: All five viscera receive their qi from the stomach; the stomach is the root of the five viscera. The qi of the viscera cannot reach the Hand Taiyin channel by itself — it must rely on the qi of the stomach in order to reach the Hand Taiyin. Hence each of the five viscera, at its proper season, arrives of itself at the Hand Taiyin only by means of stomach qi. Therefore, when pathogenic qi prevails, the essential qi declines; and when a disease is severe, the stomach qi cannot accompany the visceral qi in reaching the Hand Taiyin together — so the qi of the true viscus appears alone. When it appears alone, the disease has overcome the viscus, and hence death is said to follow. Huangdi said: Good.

Huangdi said: Whenever treating disease, one must examine the body's physique and qi, the complexion and its luster, the strength or weakness of the pulse, and whether the disease is new or old, and then treat it without letting the proper moment pass. When physique and qi correspond to each other, this is called treatable; when the complexion has a floating luster, this is called easily cured; when the pulse follows the pattern of the four seasons, this is called treatable; when the pulse is weak yet smooth, this indicates the presence of stomach qi, called easily treated, and one should select the treatment according to the season. When physique and qi fail to correspond, this is called difficult to treat; when the complexion is withered and without luster, this is called difficult to cure; when the pulse is full and hard, this is called worsening; when the pulse runs contrary to the four seasons, this is called untreatable. One must examine these four difficulties and state them clearly. What is meant by "running contrary to the four seasons" is: in spring obtaining the lung pulse, in summer obtaining the kidney pulse, in autumn obtaining the heart pulse, in winter obtaining the spleen pulse — when these arrive suspended, cut off, sunken, and rough, this is called running contrary to the four seasons. Even when there is not yet a visible visceral form of disease, if in spring and summer the pulse is sunken and rough, or in autumn and winter the pulse is floating and large, this too is called running contrary to the four seasons. When there is heat disease yet the pulse is calm; when there is diarrhea yet the pulse is large; when blood has been lost yet the pulse is full; when the disease is internal yet the pulse is full and hard; when the disease is external yet the pulse is not full and hard — in all these cases, the disease is difficult to treat.

Huangdi said: I have heard that deficiency and excess are used to determine death and survival; I would like to hear the particulars. Qibo said: The five excesses bring death, and the five deficiencies bring death. Huangdi said: I would like to hear of the five excesses and five deficiencies. Qibo said: A full pulse, hot skin, a distended abdomen, blockage of both the front and back passages, and oppression with clouded vision — these are called the five excesses. A fine pulse, cold skin, insufficient qi, diarrhea and incontinence of both front and back passages, and inability to take in food and drink — these are called the five deficiencies. Huangdi said: Yet sometimes at such times a person survives — why is that? Qibo said: If thin gruel enters the stomach and the diarrhea stops, then even one with deficiency will live; if the body sweats and the bowels move freely afterward, then even one with excess will live. These are the signs that indicate survival.

title_en: Treatise on the Three Sections and Nine Indicators
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: I have learned from you, Master, about the nine needles — so many and so vast that they cannot be exhausted. I wish to hear the essential Way, so that I may bequeath it to my sons and grandsons, transmit it to later generations, inscribe it in my very marrow, and store it within my liver and lungs; I will receive it with a blood oath and dare not disclose it carelessly. Let it accord with the Way of Heaven, having necessarily a beginning and an end, corresponding above to the brilliance of Heaven, the stars, and the calendrical order, and matching below the four seasons and the five phases, with noble and humble alternating in turn, winter belonging to yin and summer to yang — how does the human being correspond to this? I wish to hear the method. Qibo replied: How subtle a question this is! This concerns the utmost calculations of Heaven and Earth. Huangdi said: I wish to hear how the utmost calculations of Heaven and Earth correspond to the human form, so that blood and qi may be made to flow freely, and death and survival determined — how is this done? Qibo said: The utmost calculations of Heaven and Earth begin at one and end at nine. One is Heaven, two is Earth, three is Man; multiplying this by three, three times three makes nine, corresponding to the nine fields of Heaven. Therefore the human being has three sections, and each section has three indicators, by which death and survival are determined, the hundred diseases are managed, deficiency and excess are regulated, and pathogenic illness is removed.

Huangdi said: What are meant by the three sections? Qibo said: There is a lower section, a middle section, and an upper section; each section has three indicators. The three indicators are Heaven, Earth, and Man — one must point to them and guide the examination in order to obtain the true reading. In the upper section, Heaven is the pulsing vessel at the two temples; the Earth of the upper section is the pulsing vessel at the two temples; the Man of the upper section is the pulsing vessel in front of the ears. In the middle section, Heaven is the Hand Taiyin channel; the Earth of the middle section is the Hand Yangming channel; the Man of the middle section is the Hand Shaoyin channel. In the lower section, Heaven is the Foot Jueyin channel; the Earth of the lower section is the Foot Shaoyin channel; the Man of the lower section is the Foot Taiyin channel. Thus, the Heaven of the lower section indicates the liver, its Earth indicates the kidney, and its Man indicates the qi of the spleen and stomach. Huangdi said: What of the indicators of the middle section? Qibo said: There too are Heaven, Earth, and Man. Heaven indicates the lung, Earth indicates the qi within the chest, and Man indicates the heart. Huangdi said: By what are the indicators of the upper section determined? Qibo said: There too are Heaven, Earth, and Man. Heaven indicates the qi of the head and temples, Earth indicates the qi of the mouth and teeth, and Man indicates the qi of the ears and eyes. Thus each of the three sections has its Heaven, its Earth, and its Man. Three make a Heaven, three make an Earth, three make a Man; multiplying three by three gives nine in total; the nine are divided into the nine fields, and the nine fields correspond to the nine viscera. Hence there are five spirit-viscera and four form-viscera, together making the nine viscera. When the five viscera have already failed, the complexion will necessarily be withered, and when withered, death necessarily follows. Huangdi said: How does one determine this? Qibo said: One must first gauge whether the body's physique is fat or thin, in order to regulate the deficiency or excess of its qi — if there is excess, drain it; if there is deficiency, supplement it. One must first clear the blood vessels and only then regulate the qi, regardless of what the disease is, taking balance as the goal.

Huangdi said: How does one determine death and survival? Qibo said: When the physique is full but the pulse fine, and the breath insufficient to sustain respiration, this is dangerous. When the physique is thin but the pulse large, and there is excess qi within the chest, this means death. When physique and qi correspond to one another, this means life. When the pulses are irregular and uncoordinated, this means disease. When the three sections and nine indicators all fail to correspond with one another, this means death. When the pulses above and below, left and right, respond to each other like the alternating strokes of a pestle pounding grain, the disease is severe. When above and below, left and right, are so out of correspondence that they cannot be counted together, this means death. When the indicator of the middle section, though regulated by itself, fails to correspond with the other viscera, this means death. When the indicators of the middle section diminish relative to one another, this means death. When the eyes are sunken inward, this means death. Huangdi said: How does one know where the disease is located? Qibo said: When examining the nine indicators, if one alone is small, there is disease; if one alone is large, there is disease; if one alone is rapid, there is disease; if one alone is slow, there is disease; if one alone is hot, there is disease; if one alone is cold, there is disease; if one alone is sunken and depressed, there is disease. Press with the left hand on the leg, five inches above the ankle, and then flick near the ankle with the right hand — if the response travels upward beyond five inches in a wriggling motion, there is no disease; if the response is rapid, and under the hand feels turbid and rolling, there is disease; if under the hand it feels sluggish and slow, there is disease; if the response does not reach up to five inches, or flicking produces no response at all, this means death. Hence, when the flesh has wasted away and the body cannot move, this means death. When the middle section is now sparse and now rapid, this means death. When the pulse is intermittent and hooked, the disease lies in the network vessels. In the mutual correspondence of the nine indicators, above and below should be as one, and must not fail to correspond. When one indicator lags, there is disease; when two indicators lag, the disease is severe; when three indicators lag, the disease is critical. What is meant by "lagging" is that the responses are not simultaneous. Examine the bowels and viscera in order to know the timing of death and survival.

One must first understand the channels and vessels, and only then can one understand the pulse of disease. When the pulse of the true viscus appears, the disease overcomes the person and death results. When the qi of the Foot Taiyang channel is cut off, the feet cannot be flexed or extended, and at death the eyes will necessarily roll upward. Huangdi said: How is it that winter belongs to yin and summer to yang? Qibo said: Among the pulses of the nine indicators, those that are sunken, fine, suspended, and cut off belong to yin, governed by winter, and hence death occurs at midnight. Those that are full, agitated, panting, and rapid belong to yang, governed by summer, and hence death occurs at midday. Therefore, in cold-heat disease, death occurs at dawn. In internal heat and heat disease, death occurs at midday. In wind disease, death occurs at dusk. In water disease, death occurs at midnight. When the pulse is now sparse, now rapid, now slow, now hasty, death occurs at the turning of the four seasons. If the flesh has already wasted away, even though the nine indicators are regulated, death still occurs. If the seven signs of fatal prognosis appear but the nine indicators all comply with the norm, the patient does not die. What is meant by "not dying" refers to diseases of wind qi and diseases that run their course over a month, which resemble the diseases of the seven signs but are not truly so — hence it is said they do not die. But if the seven signs of disease are truly present and the pulse indicators are also in decay, then death follows, and hiccup and belching will necessarily occur. One must carefully inquire into how the disease began and what is presently afflicting the patient, and only then, in turn, palpate and trace each pulse, observing whether the channels and network vessels are floating or sunken, and follow them up and down, with the flow or against it. If the pulse is brisk, there is no disease; if the pulse is slow, there is disease; if the pulse neither comes nor goes, this means death; if the skin adheres, wasted and inelastic, this means death.

Huangdi said: How does one treat the cases that can be treated? Qibo said: When the disease is in the main channels, treat the main channels; when the disease is in the grandchild network vessels, treat the blood of the grandchild network vessels; when the disease is in the blood and the body has pain, treat the channels and network vessels. When the disease lies in an unusual pathogen, needle the unusual pathogen's vessel using contralateral needling. When the pathogen lingers and wastes the flesh without moving, needle at the joints. When there is excess above and deficiency below, palpate and follow it, seek out the knotted network vessels, needle them to let out the blood, and thereby open the passage so that it can be seen to flow freely. When the pupils are raised upward, this is insufficiency of the Taiyang; when the eyes roll fully upward and fixed, the Taiyang qi has already been cut off — this is essential to determining death and survival, and must not go unexamined. At the fingers, and five finger-widths above the outer ankle of the wrist, retain the needle.

title_en: Treatise on Variances in the Channels and Vessels
paragraphs_en: Huangdi asked: Do a person's dwelling and posture, movement and rest, courage and timidity also cause the pulse to change accordingly? Qibo replied: In general, a person's fright, fear, anger, exertion, movement, and rest all cause change. Thus, when walking at night, panting arises from the kidney, and the overflowing qi brings disease to the lung. When there is a fall causing fear, panting arises from the liver, and the overflowing qi harms the spleen. When there is fright and fear, panting arises from the lung, and the overflowing qi injures the heart. When fording water and stumbling, panting arises from the kidney and the bones; at such times, in the courageous the qi moves on and the matter ends there, but in the timid it lodges and becomes disease. Hence it is said: the way of diagnosing disease is to observe a person's courage or timidity, and the state of the bones, flesh, and skin — being able to know these particulars constitutes the method of diagnosis. Thus, when eating and drinking to excessive fullness, sweat issues from the stomach. When startled so that essence is lost, sweat issues from the heart. When carrying heavy loads on a long journey, sweat issues from the kidney. When running swiftly in fear, sweat issues from the liver. When exerting the body in toil, sweat issues from the spleen. Therefore, throughout spring, autumn, winter, and summer, the yin and yang of the four seasons — disease arises from excessive use, and this is the constant rule.

When the qi of food enters the stomach, its essence is dispersed to the liver, and the overflowing qi extends to the sinews. The overflowing qi also enters the stomach; its turbid qi returns to the heart, and its overflowing essence extends to the vessels. The qi of the vessels flows through the channels, and the qi of the channels returns to the lung; the lung gathers all the vessels as at court and transports essence to the skin and body hair. The qi of the hair and vessels unite their essence and move qi to the bowels. The essence-qi of the bowels, luminous and clear, is retained in the four viscera, and this qi returns to the balance-beam of the pulse. When the balance is even, the qi opening at the wrist measures its proper inch, by which death and survival are determined. When drink enters the stomach, its overflowing essence-qi is transported upward to the spleen. The spleen qi disperses the essence, sending it upward to the lung, which regulates and clears the waterways, sending it downward to the bladder. The essence of water is thereby distributed to the four quarters, and the five channels move together in accord with it, in accordance with the four seasons and the yin and yang of the five viscera — estimating and measuring these is the constant standard.

When the Taiyang viscus-pulse arrives alone, there is reversal, panting, and deficient qi rebelling upward — this is insufficiency of yin and surplus of yang; both the exterior and interior should be drained together, taking points at the lower transport points. When the Yangming viscus-pulse arrives alone, this is a doubling and compounding of yang qi; one should drain the yang and supplement the yin, taking points at the lower transport points. When the Shaoyang viscus-pulse arrives alone, this is reversal qi, with the pulse at the yin heel vessel in front suddenly enlarged; take points at the lower transport points — when Shaoyang arrives alone, this is an excess of the one yang. When the Taiyin viscus-pulse beats abnormally, one must apply the mind carefully to discern the truth of the case: when the qi of the five pulses is scant and the stomach qi is not level, this is the three yin; one should treat the lower transport points, supplementing yang and draining yin. When the one yang howls alone, this is reversal of the Shaoyang; yang is doubled above, the four vessels contend and stretch taut, and the qi returns to the kidney; one should treat the channels and network vessels, draining yang and supplementing yin. When the one yin arrives, this is a matter of the Jueyin; there is true deficiency with a painful oppression at the heart, reversal qi lingering and pressing, manifesting as pale, cold sweat; regulate the diet and harmonize the medicine, with treatment at the lower transport points.

Huangdi said: What is the image of the Taiyang viscus-pulse? Qibo said: It resembles the three yang, and is floating. Huangdi said: What is the image of the Shaoyang viscus-pulse? Qibo said: It resembles the one yang; the one yang viscus-pulse is smooth and slippery, yet not full. Huangdi said: What is the image of the Yangming viscus-pulse? Qibo said: It resembles being large and floating. The beating of the Taiyin viscus-pulse is described as hidden yet drumming. When the two yin beat and arrive, this is the kidney, sunken and not floating.
